name: English I
description: Foundations of English language arts — reading literary and informational texts, grammar, vocabulary, and writing across narrative, descriptive, expository, and argumentative modes.

modules.title: Reading Comprehension Strategies
modules.objectives: Identify main idea and supporting details; make inferences from text; recognize common text structures; summarize literary and informational passages accurately.
modules.readingNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: Purdue OWL — Open writing and grammar exercises: Reading Comprehension Strategies.
modules.assignment: Read one short informational article from a free news, museum, library, or government education site and one short public-domain story from a source such as Project Gutenberg or a teacher-provided packet. For each: (1) write a one-paragraph summary in your own words, (2) identify the main idea and three supporting details, (3) make two inferences supported with text evidence, and (4) identify the text structure used. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: drafts, annotations, quotations, analysis, or revised writing from the Reading Comprehension Strategies module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Parts of Speech & Grammar Foundations
modules.objectives: Identify and correctly use the eight parts of speech — nouns, pronouns, verbs, adjectives, adverbs, prepositions, conjunctions, and interjections — in sentences. Students will analyze model texts, draft or revise written responses, and support claims with textual evidence in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: Purdue OWL — Parts of Speech Overview
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Write 10 original sentences (at least one per part of speech). Then label every word in each sentence with its part of speech. Finally, find a paragraph from a book or article you are reading and annotate each word with its part of speech. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: drafts, annotations, quotations, analysis, or revised writing from the Parts of Speech & Grammar Foundations module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Sentence Structure & Syntax
modules.objectives: Identify and write simple, compound, complex, and compound-complex sentences; recognize and correct sentence fragments and run-on sentences; use appropriate conjunctions and punctuation.
modules.readingNote: Purdue OWL — writing and grammar resources for reviewing sentence structure and syntax.
modules.videoNote: Purdue OWL — open writing resource for reviewing sentence patterns and clarity.
modules.practiceNote: Purdue OWL — grammar exercises; complete the GIIS Learn Portal assignment for submitted practice.
modules.assignment: Write 5 examples of each sentence type (simple, compound, complex, compound-complex) on topics of your choice. Then revise 10 provided fragments and run-ons into correct sentences. Explain what made each original sentence incorrect. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: drafts, annotations, quotations, analysis, or revised writing from the Sentence Structure & Syntax module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Vocabulary in Context & Word Study
modules.objectives: Use context clues to determine word meanings; analyze Greek and Latin roots, prefixes, and suffixes to decode unfamiliar words; distinguish between connotation and denotation.
modules.readingNote: Purdue OWL — Word Choice: Connotation and Denotation
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Choose 15 unfamiliar words from your current reading. For each: (1) write the sentence from the text, (2) use context clues to infer the meaning, (3) identify any roots/prefixes/suffixes, (4) look up the dictionary definition, and (5) write a sentence using the word with a positive vs. negative connotation. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: drafts, annotations, quotations, analysis, or revised writing from the Vocabulary in Context & Word Study module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Narrative Writing
modules.objectives: Understand story structure (exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, resolution); identify and apply point of view; use narrative techniques such as dialogue, pacing, and description; plan and draft a personal narrative.
modules.readingNote: Purdue OWL — The Writing Process (planning, drafting, revising for narrative)
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: Purdue OWL — Open writing and grammar exercises: Narrative Writing.
modules.assignment: Write a personal narrative of at least 500 words about a meaningful experience. Include: a clear story arc (all 5 plot stages), first-person point of view, at least three instances of dialogue, sensory details, and a reflection on what the experience meant to you. Submit a pre-writing outline with your draft. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: drafts, annotations, quotations, analysis, or revised writing from the Narrative Writing module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Descriptive Writing
modules.objectives: Use precise sensory details (sight, sound, smell, taste, touch) to create vivid descriptions; employ figurative language including simile, metaphor, and personification effectively. Students will analyze model texts, draft or revise written responses, and support claims with textual evidence in the module assignment.
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: Purdue OWL — Open writing and grammar exercises: Descriptive Writing.
modules.assignment: Write two descriptive paragraphs (each at least 150 words): one describing a place and one describing a person. Each paragraph must include at least two similes, one metaphor, one instance of personification, and details from at least three of the five senses. Underline and label each figurative language device. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: drafts, annotations, quotations, analysis, or revised writing from the Descriptive Writing module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Short Story Analysis
modules.objectives: Analyze the elements of fiction: plot (stages), character (protagonist, antagonist, motivation), setting (time, place, atmosphere), conflict (internal vs. external), and theme (universal message). Students will analyze model texts, draft or revise written responses, and support claims with textual evidence in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: Project Gutenberg — The Gift of the Magi by O. Henry, a public-domain short story suitable for plot, character, setting, conflict, and theme analysis.
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Read one teacher-approved public-domain short story, such as a story from Project Gutenberg, the Library of Congress, or an instructor-provided open packet. Write a 400–500 word literary analysis that identifies: (1) each stage of the plot, (2) the protagonist and antagonist with their motivations, (3) the setting and its role in the story, (4) the central conflict (type and description), and (5) at least two themes with supporting evidence from the text. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: drafts, annotations, quotations, analysis, or revised writing from the Short Story Analysis module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Poetry: Forms & Interpretation
modules.objectives: Identify elements of poetry including rhyme scheme, meter, stanza, tone, and imagery; analyze lyric and narrative poems; interpret figurative language and theme in poetry.
modules.readingNote: Purdue OWL — Introduction to Poetry: Forms and Elements
modules.videoNote: Crash Course Literature — Poetry, Rhyme, and Meter
modules.video2Note: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Analyze two poems of your choice (at least one must be a lyric poem and one a narrative poem). For each poem write a 200–300 word analysis covering: rhyme scheme (labelled ABAB etc.), type and effect of meter, tone and how it shifts, at least two examples of imagery, and the central theme. Include the poem text with your annotations. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: drafts, annotations, quotations, analysis, or revised writing from the Poetry: Forms & Interpretation module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Drama & Theater Texts
modules.objectives: Read and interpret a play script; understand stage directions and their purpose; analyze dialogue as character and plot development; identify dramatic irony and its effect on the audience.
modules.readingNote: Project Gutenberg — Hamlet by William Shakespeare, public-domain play text for studying stage directions, dialogue, dramatic irony, and script interpretation.
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Read one teacher-approved public-domain drama excerpt, such as a scene from Shakespeare or another open play text. Write a 350–450 word response that: (1) explains what the stage directions reveal about setting and mood, (2) analyzes how dialogue reveals character, (3) identifies one example of dramatic irony and explains the effect on the audience, and (4) compares how reading a play differs from reading a short story. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: drafts, annotations, quotations, analysis, or revised writing from the Drama & Theater Texts module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Novel Study: Character & Theme
modules.objectives: Trace character development across a novel; distinguish static from dynamic characters; identify and analyze major themes; use textual evidence to support literary analysis.
modules.readingNote: Project Gutenberg — The Yellow Wallpaper by Charlotte Perkins Gilman, a public-domain short story with strong character development and theme evidence.
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Read one teacher-approved public-domain or openly provided short story with a clear central character. Write a 500-word character analysis essay: (1) trace the character's development from beginning to end with at least four quoted examples, (2) classify the character as static or dynamic and justify, (3) identify two major themes and explain how the character's journey illustrates each, and (4) explain what the author may be saying about the human experience. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: drafts, annotations, quotations, analysis, or revised writing from the Novel Study: Character & Theme module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Argumentative Writing & Evidence
modules.objectives: Understand the structure of an argument (claim, evidence, reasoning); write a clear thesis statement; incorporate and cite evidence; address counterarguments and write a rebuttal. Students will analyze model texts, draft or revise written responses, and support claims with textual evidence in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: Purdue OWL — Argumentative Essays: Structure and Strategy
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Write a 4–5 paragraph argumentative essay on a topic of your choice (e.g., school uniforms, social media age limits, year-round school). Include: a thesis statement, two body paragraphs each with a claim + two pieces of evidence + reasoning, one counterargument paragraph with a rebuttal, and a conclusion. Underline your thesis and circle your evidence. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: drafts, annotations, quotations, analysis, or revised writing from the Argumentative Writing & Evidence module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Expository & Informational Writing
modules.objectives: Write expository essays using compare/contrast, cause/effect, and problem/solution organizational structures; use appropriate transitions; maintain formal, objective tone. Students will analyze model texts, draft or revise written responses, and support claims with textual evidence in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: Purdue OWL — Expository Essays: Types and Structure
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: Purdue OWL — Transition Words and Phrases for Expository Writing
modules.assignment: Write three short expository paragraphs (150 words each), one using each structure: (1) compare/contrast two books, films, or historical events you have studied, (2) cause/effect of a social or natural phenomenon, (3) problem/solution for a school or community issue. Each paragraph must include at least three appropriate transition words, which you should underline. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: drafts, annotations, quotations, analysis, or revised writing from the Expository & Informational Writing module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Research Skills & MLA Basics
modules.objectives: Evaluate sources for credibility using CRAAP criteria; distinguish paraphrasing from quoting; incorporate in-text citations; format a Works Cited page in MLA style. Students will analyze model texts, draft or revise written responses, and support claims with textual evidence in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: Purdue OWL — MLA General Format and Works Cited Guide
modules.videoNote: Purdue OWL — Open writing lesson resource for this topic: Research Skills & MLA Basics.
modules.practiceNote: Purdue OWL — MLA Works Cited: Books, Articles, and Websites (with examples)
modules.assignment: Research a topic of your choice (minimum 3 sources: one book/ebook, one website, one article). Write a 300-word informational paragraph that paraphrases at least two sources and directly quotes at least one, all with correct MLA in-text citations. Attach a properly formatted MLA Works Cited page with all three sources. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: drafts, annotations, quotations, analysis, or revised writing from the Research Skills & MLA Basics module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

questions.question: Which strategy involves identifying what a text is mostly about, stated in one or two sentences?
questions.explanation: The main idea is the central point of a passage, stated concisely. Summarizing covers the whole passage; inference draws conclusions not stated directly.

questions.question: A reader concludes that a character feels guilty based on the character's nervous behavior, even though guilt is never stated. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: An inference is a conclusion drawn from text evidence plus background knowledge, not stated explicitly in the text.

questions.question: In the sentence 'The exhausted hikers slowly climbed the steep mountain,' the word 'slowly' is a(n):
questions.explanation: 'Slowly' modifies the verb 'climbed,' describing how the action is performed. Words that modify verbs are adverbs.

questions.question: Which sentence is a compound sentence?
options: She wanted coffee, but the cafe was closed.
The quiet cafe on the corner.
questions.answer: She wanted coffee, but the cafe was closed.
questions.explanation: A compound sentence joins two independent clauses. This sentence joins "She wanted coffee" and "the cafe was closed" with the coordinating conjunction "but."

questions.question: The word 'incredible' contains the prefix 'in-' meaning 'not' and the root 'cred' meaning 'believe.' What does 'incredible' most likely mean?
questions.explanation: Using prefix meaning 'not' + root 'cred' (believe): 'incredible' = not believable, or so extraordinary as to be hard to believe.

questions.question: Which sentence contains a sentence fragment?
options: The dog barked loudly all night.
She finished her homework before dinner.
They arrived early and found good seats.
questions.explanation: 'Because the storm was coming' is a dependent clause — it cannot stand alone as a sentence because it begins with a subordinating conjunction and has no independent clause to complete it.

questions.question: In narrative writing, which stage of plot structure introduces the main characters and setting?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Exposition.

questions.question: Which sentence contains a simile?
options: The moon is a silver lantern.
Her smile was as bright as the sun.
The wind whispered through the trees.
He ran quickly down the street.
questions.answer: Her smile was as bright as the sun.
questions.explanation: A simile compares two unlike things using "like" or "as." "As bright as the sun" is a simile.

questions.question: In 'The Lottery' by Shirley Jackson, the lottery is revealed to be a ritualized stoning. This shocking ending is best described as which type of conflict?
questions.explanation: The central conflict is between the individual and the traditions/norms of the village community — a classic person vs. society conflict.

questions.question: What is the denotation of the word 'home'?
options: A place of warmth, love, and safety
A building where a person lives
A feeling of belonging and comfort
questions.answer: A building where a person lives
questions.explanation: Denotation is the literal, dictionary definition of a word. 'Home' literally means a place where one lives. The other options describe connotations — emotional associations.

questions.question: Which point of view uses 'I' and 'me' and is told from the narrator's own perspective?
questions.explanation: First-person narration uses first-person pronouns (I, me, my, we) and tells the story from the narrator's own point of view.

questions.question: A literary theme is best described as:
options: A list of events in the plot
A universal message or insight about life conveyed by the story
The physical location where the story takes place
questions.answer: A universal message or insight about life conveyed by the story
questions.explanation: A theme is a larger message or insight developed through characters, conflict, and events.

questions.question: Which figurative language device gives human qualities to a non-human thing?
questions.explanation: Personification attributes human characteristics (thoughts, emotions, actions) to animals, objects, or abstract ideas. Example: 'The trees danced in the wind.'

questions.question: Identify one internal conflict and one external conflict that could exist in the same story. Give an example of each.
questions.answer: Internal: person vs. self (e.g., a character struggling with guilt or a difficult decision). External: person vs. person, vs. society, vs. nature, or vs. technology (e.g., a character fighting an antagonist or surviving a storm).
questions.explanation: Internal conflict occurs within a character's mind; external conflict occurs between a character and an outside force.

questions.question: Write an original complex sentence using a subordinating conjunction, and identify the independent clause and the dependent clause.
questions.answer: Example: 'Although the test was difficult, she remained calm.' Independent clause: 'she remained calm.' Dependent clause: 'Although the test was difficult.'
questions.explanation: A complex sentence contains one independent clause and at least one dependent clause introduced by a subordinating conjunction (although, because, since, when, etc.).

questions.question: A text lists causes of the American Revolution using signal words such as "first," "second," and "additionally." Which text structure is being used?
questions.explanation: Signal words such as "first," "second," and "additionally" show that ideas are being presented in order as a sequence or list.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a run-on sentence?
options: After the rain stopped, we went outside.
We went to the park we had a great time.
She studied hard for the test.
questions.answer: We went to the park we had a great time.
questions.explanation: A run-on sentence incorrectly joins two independent clauses without proper punctuation or a conjunction. 'We went to the park' and 'we had a great time' are fused without a comma or conjunction.

questions.question: In poetry, the repetition of the same end sounds in two or more lines is called:
questions.explanation: Rhyme is the repetition of similar end sounds in lines of poetry (e.g., 'day' and 'way'). Meter refers to the rhythmic pattern of stressed and unstressed syllables.

questions.question: Stage directions in a play script serve primarily to:
options: Tell actors how to move and speak, and describe the setting
List the themes of the play
Indicate which characters are most important
questions.answer: Tell actors how to move and speak, and describe the setting
questions.explanation: Stage directions (written in italics or brackets) guide actors' movements and expressions and describe the physical setting, props, and atmosphere of the scene.

questions.question: A dynamic character is one who:
options: Narrates the story in first person
Undergoes significant change during the story
questions.answer: Undergoes significant change during the story
questions.explanation: A dynamic character changes in an important way as a result of the story's events.

questions.question: In an argumentative essay, a counterargument is most effectively followed by:
options: Another supporting point for your claim
A rebuttal that explains why your original claim still stands
A quotation from the opposing side
questions.answer: A rebuttal that explains why your original claim still stands
questions.explanation: After acknowledging a counterargument, an effective writer provides a rebuttal — a response that refutes the opposing view and reinforces the writer's own claim.

questions.question: Which organizational structure would work best for an essay explaining why school lunches should be healthier?
questions.explanation: The essay identifies a problem (unhealthy school lunches) and proposes solutions (improvements). Problem-solution structure is ideal for this type of argument.

questions.question: Which of the following is the BEST thesis statement for an argumentative essay?
options: Social media is used by billions of people around the world.
Some people think social media is good; others think it is bad.
Social media companies should be required to implement age verification to protect children from harmful content.
This essay will discuss the pros and cons of social media.
questions.answer: Social media companies should be required to implement age verification to protect children from harmful content.
questions.explanation: An effective thesis makes a specific, arguable claim. The other options are facts, vague observations, or announcements of topic — not debatable positions.

questions.question: In the CRAAP test for evaluating sources, the letters stand for Currency, Relevance, Authority, Accuracy, and:
questions.explanation: The CRAAP test evaluates Currency, Relevance, Authority, Accuracy, and Purpose.

questions.question: Which is an example of correct MLA in-text citation for a direct quote?
questions.explanation: MLA in-text citations use the author's last name and page number in parentheses with no comma: (Jackson 3). APA style uses a comma and 'p.' — not MLA.

questions.question: Situational irony occurs when:
options: The outcome is very different from what was expected
The audience knows something a character does not
A speaker says the opposite of what they mean
A character directly explains the theme
questions.answer: The outcome is very different from what was expected
questions.explanation: Situational irony depends on a surprising contrast between what readers expect to happen and what actually happens.

questions.question: In 'Flowers for Algernon,' Charlie Gordon changes from having an intellectual disability to genius-level intelligence and then declines again. He is best classified as a:
questions.explanation: Charlie undergoes profound psychological and intellectual change throughout the story, making him a dynamic character — one who is significantly transformed by the events of the narrative.

questions.question: During revision, which level of change should usually be addressed first?
questions.explanation: Writers usually revise larger issues such as ideas, organization, and clarity before editing sentence-level mechanics.

questions.question: Which sentence uses the word 'snake' with a negative connotation?
options: The snake slid smoothly across the warm rock.
The biologist studied the snake's scales.
He was a snake who betrayed all his friends.
The children learned about snakes at the zoo.
questions.answer: He was a snake who betrayed all his friends.
questions.explanation: Using 'snake' to describe a person who betrays others carries negative connotations of deceit and treachery, going beyond the literal meaning of the animal.

questions.question: A poem with the rhyme scheme ABAB CDCD EFEF GG is most likely a:
questions.explanation: The Shakespearean (or English) sonnet follows the rhyme scheme ABAB CDCD EFEF GG — three quatrains and a closing couplet, totaling 14 lines.

questions.question: Which transition word signals a contrast between two ideas?
questions.explanation: 'However' signals a contrasting or opposing idea. 'Furthermore' and 'in addition' add information; 'consequently' shows cause and effect.

questions.question: In the sentence "The ancient, crumbling castle loomed over the foggy valley," what mood does the descriptive language most strongly create?
questions.explanation: Words such as "ancient," "crumbling," "loomed," and "foggy" create a dark, uneasy mood.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct way to paraphrase a source?
options: Copy the original text word for word and add quotation marks.
Change a few words from the original while keeping the same sentence structure.
Restate the author's ideas entirely in your own words and sentence structure.
Summarize the whole article in one word.
questions.answer: Restate the author's ideas entirely in your own words and sentence structure.
questions.explanation: Paraphrasing means expressing another person's ideas in your own words and structure — not copying the original or just replacing a few words (which is plagiarism).

questions.question: Explain the difference between a metaphor and a simile. Give one original example of each.
questions.answer: A simile compares two things using 'like' or 'as' (e.g., 'Her voice was like music'). A metaphor states directly that one thing IS another without using 'like' or 'as' (e.g., 'Her voice was music to my ears').
questions.explanation: Both are figures of speech that make comparisons, but similes use 'like' or 'as' while metaphors make a direct identification between two unlike things.

questions.question: What is the difference between revising and editing? Name one task you would do during revision and one task you would do during editing.
questions.answer: Revising focuses on content, ideas, and organization (e.g., adding evidence, reorganizing paragraphs). Editing focuses on correctness at the surface level (e.g., fixing grammar, spelling, and punctuation errors).
questions.explanation: Revision is a global process of improving meaning and structure; editing is a local process of correcting errors in mechanics and language conventions.

quizQuestions.question: What is the main idea of a passage?
options: The first sentence of the passage
The most important point the author wants to convey
A specific detail that supports the author's argument
quizQuestions.answer: The most important point the author wants to convey
quizQuestions.explanation: The main idea is the central, overarching point of the passage — what the entire text is mostly about.

quizQuestions.question: In Reading Comprehension Strategies, Making an inference means:
options: Looking up a word in the dictionary
Drawing a conclusion from clues in the text and your own knowledge
Copying a sentence directly from the text
Finding the title of the text
quizQuestions.answer: Drawing a conclusion from clues in the text and your own knowledge
quizQuestions.explanation: An inference is a logical conclusion based on text evidence combined with prior knowledge — going beyond what is directly stated.

quizQuestions.question: Which option best states the main idea of a passage?
options: A specific detail that supports the author's argument
The first sentence of the passage
The most important point the author wants to convey
quizQuestions.answer: The most important point the author wants to convey
quizQuestions.explanation: The best answer is The most important point the author wants to convey. It directly fits the prompt, while the other choices reflect different course ideas, incomplete definitions, or common mistakes.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Which signal word indicates a cause-and-effect text structure?
quizQuestions.answer: Therefore
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "Therefore" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: In the sentence 'They quickly ran to the bright red car,' which word is a pronoun?
quizQuestions.answer: They
quizQuestions.explanation: 'They' is a personal pronoun that replaces a noun (the names of specific people). Pronouns stand in for nouns.

quizQuestions.question: Which part of speech modifies a noun or pronoun?
quizQuestions.answer: Adjective
quizQuestions.explanation: Adjectives describe or modify nouns and pronouns, answering questions like 'which one?', 'what kind?', and 'how many?'

quizQuestions.question: In the sentence 'She placed the book on the table,' the word 'on' is a:
quizQuestions.answer: Preposition
quizQuestions.explanation: 'On' shows the relationship between 'book' and 'table,' indicating position. Prepositions show relationships of place, time, or direction.

quizQuestions.question: In the sentence "They quickly ran to the bright red car," which word is a pronoun?
quizQuestions.answer: They
quizQuestions.explanation: "They" is a pronoun because it takes the place of a noun or noun phrase. This reinforces the Parts of Speech & Grammar Foundations idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a simple sentence?
options: Although he was tired, he finished his work.
Because it rained, the game was cancelled, and we went home.
quizQuestions.answer: She sang and danced.
quizQuestions.explanation: A simple sentence has one independent clause. 'She sang and danced' has one subject ('She') and a compound predicate ('sang and danced') — still one independent clause.

quizQuestions.question: A complex sentence contains:
options: Two independent clauses joined by a comma
One independent clause and one or more dependent clauses
Only a subject and a verb
Two independent clauses and two dependent clauses
quizQuestions.answer: One independent clause and one or more dependent clauses
quizQuestions.explanation: A complex sentence has one independent clause (can stand alone) plus one or more dependent clauses (introduced by subordinating conjunctions like 'although,' 'because,' 'when,' etc.).

quizQuestions.question: Identify the type of sentence: 'She loves to read, and her brother prefers to draw.'
quizQuestions.answer: Compound
quizQuestions.explanation: A compound sentence joins two independent clauses with a coordinating conjunction. 'She loves to read' + 'and' + 'her brother prefers to draw' = compound sentence.

quizQuestions.question: Read this sentence: 'The frugal man never spent money unnecessarily.' Using context clues, 'frugal' most likely means:
quizQuestions.answer: Careful and sparing with money
quizQuestions.explanation: The context clue 'never spent money unnecessarily' suggests the person is careful with money. 'Frugal' means economical, avoiding waste.

quizQuestions.question: The prefix 'mis-' in 'misunderstand' means:
quizQuestions.answer: Not or wrongly
quizQuestions.explanation: The prefix 'mis-' means wrong or badly (misunderstand = understand wrongly). Other examples: misspell, misuse, mislead.

quizQuestions.question: The Latin root 'port' means 'carry.' Which of the following words comes from this root?
quizQuestions.answer: Transport
quizQuestions.explanation: 'Transport' = trans (across) + port (carry) = carry across. Other 'port' root words: import, export, portable, report.

quizQuestions.question: Which sentence uses the word 'cheap' with a negative connotation?
options: This store sells cheap goods at low prices.
He found a cheap deal on airline tickets.
She is too cheap to leave a tip at the restaurant.
The thrift store has cheap clothing options.
quizQuestions.answer: She is too cheap to leave a tip at the restaurant.
quizQuestions.explanation: Here 'cheap' is used to suggest the person is stingy or selfish — a negative social judgment, not just a neutral reference to low cost.

quizQuestions.question: In narrative story structure, the climax is:
options: The moment of highest tension or the turning point
quizQuestions.answer: The moment of highest tension or the turning point
quizQuestions.explanation: The climax is the most exciting or intense moment in the story — the point at which the central conflict reaches its peak and begins to resolve.

quizQuestions.question: Which point of view uses 'you' and directly addresses the reader?
quizQuestions.answer: Second person
quizQuestions.explanation: Second-person narration uses 'you' to address the reader directly, as if placing them in the story. It is uncommon in fiction but used in instruction and some experimental literature.

quizQuestions.question: In narrative structure, the climax is:
options: The moment of highest tension or the turning point
The final reflection after the conflict ends
A description of the narrator's background
quizQuestions.answer: The moment of highest tension or the turning point
quizQuestions.explanation: The climax is the turning point or point of greatest tension in the plot. It points to the reading, grammar, or writing feature that makes the answer defensible.

quizQuestions.question: Which point of view uses "you" and directly addresses the reader?
quizQuestions.answer: Second person
quizQuestions.explanation: Second-person point of view addresses the reader directly with pronouns such as "you."

quizQuestions.question: Which sentence contains the most effective sensory detail?
options: The warm, buttery croissant crackled as she bit into its flaky layers.
There was food on the table.
quizQuestions.answer: The warm, buttery croissant crackled as she bit into its flaky layers.
quizQuestions.explanation: This sentence appeals to touch (warm), taste (buttery), sound (crackled), and texture (flaky layers) — specific, sensory language that creates a vivid impression.

quizQuestions.question: 'Life is a rollercoaster' is an example of:
quizQuestions.answer: Metaphor
quizQuestions.explanation: A metaphor directly states that one thing IS another without using 'like' or 'as.' Life is directly equated to a rollercoaster to suggest its ups and downs.

quizQuestions.question: 'The stars winked at us from the dark sky' is an example of:
quizQuestions.answer: Personification
quizQuestions.explanation: 'Winked' is a human action attributed to stars — giving a non-human thing (stars) a human quality. This is personification.

quizQuestions.question: Which sentence uses figurative language most clearly?
options: The moon was a silver coin above the quiet town.
The moon was visible at night.
She looked up at the sky.
quizQuestions.answer: The moon was a silver coin above the quiet town.
quizQuestions.explanation: This sentence uses a metaphor by comparing the moon to a silver coin, creating a clearer image for the reader.

quizQuestions.question: In a short story, the antagonist is:
options: The force or character that opposes the protagonist
The character who solves the conflict
quizQuestions.answer: The force or character that opposes the protagonist
quizQuestions.explanation: The antagonist creates obstacles for the protagonist. It can be a person, a force of nature, society, or even an internal struggle — not necessarily a 'villain.'

quizQuestions.question: Which element of fiction describes the time and place in which a story occurs?
quizQuestions.answer: Setting
quizQuestions.explanation: Setting includes the time period, physical location, and atmosphere/mood of a story. Setting can influence character behavior and the believability of the plot.

quizQuestions.question: In a short story, the setting is:
options: The central message of the story
The time and place of the action
The character who opposes the protagonist
quizQuestions.answer: The time and place of the action
quizQuestions.explanation: Setting identifies when and where a story happens, and it can shape mood, conflict, and character choices.

quizQuestions.question: What does a poem's rhyme scheme describe?
options: The number of syllables per line
The pattern of end rhymes in a poem, labeled with letters
The speed at which the poem should be read
The subject matter of the poem
quizQuestions.answer: The pattern of end rhymes in a poem, labeled with letters
quizQuestions.explanation: Rhyme scheme tracks which lines end with rhyming sounds, labeled with consecutive letters (ABAB, AABB, etc.). It reveals the poem's structure and musicality.

quizQuestions.question: In Poetry: Forms & Interpretation, A poem's tone refers to:
options: The loudness of the poem when read aloud
The author's attitude toward the subject or audience
quizQuestions.answer: The author's attitude toward the subject or audience
quizQuestions.explanation: Tone reflects the poet's emotional attitude — melancholy, celebratory, ironic, hopeful, etc. Tone is conveyed through word choice (diction) and imagery.

quizQuestions.question: A lyric poem differs from a narrative poem because a lyric poem:
options: Tells a story with characters and plot
quizQuestions.answer: Expresses personal emotions or reflections
quizQuestions.explanation: Lyric poems express the speaker's feelings, thoughts, or observations — they are personal and emotional. Narrative poems tell stories with events and characters.

quizQuestions.question: Which phrase is an example of alliteration?
options: Bright birds balanced beside the bridge
quizQuestions.answer: Bright birds balanced beside the bridge
quizQuestions.explanation: Alliteration repeats the same beginning consonant sound in nearby words, as in the repeated b sound here.

quizQuestions.question: Stage directions in a play are typically written in:
options: Italics or brackets, separate from dialogue
The first line of every scene
quizQuestions.answer: Italics or brackets, separate from dialogue
quizQuestions.explanation: Stage directions guide actors and readers about movement, expression, and setting. They are visually distinct from dialogue, typically in italics or enclosed in brackets.

quizQuestions.question: In Drama & Theater Texts, Dramatic irony occurs when:
options: The audience knows something important that a character does not
The ending is ironic and unexpected
quizQuestions.answer: The audience knows something important that a character does not
quizQuestions.explanation: Dramatic irony creates tension or suspense because the audience has knowledge that one or more characters in the play lack — they can see what the character cannot.

quizQuestions.question: How does reading a play differ from reading a novel?
options: Plays rely on dialogue and stage directions rather than a narrator's descriptions
Plays are always shorter than novels
Novels use dialogue but plays do not
quizQuestions.answer: Plays rely on dialogue and stage directions rather than a narrator's descriptions
quizQuestions.explanation: Novels use a narrator to describe scenes, thoughts, and feelings in depth. Plays convey most information through characters' dialogue and brief stage directions — the reader must infer more.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Complete this statement: A speech given by a character alone on stage, revealing their inner thoughts to the audience, is called a...
quizQuestions.answer: Soliloquy
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "Soliloquy" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: A static character is one who:
options: Changes dramatically by the end of the story
Has many layers and internal conflicts
Remains essentially unchanged throughout the story
quizQuestions.answer: Remains essentially unchanged throughout the story
quizQuestions.explanation: A static character does not experience significant change or growth. They serve a consistent role and do not undergo a meaningful transformation as a result of the story's events.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is the best piece of evidence to support a character analysis claim?
options: A general description of the story's setting
A direct quote from the text showing the character's words or actions
A summary of a different book
quizQuestions.answer: A direct quote from the text showing the character's words or actions
quizQuestions.explanation: Textual evidence — especially direct quotations showing what a character says, does, or thinks — is the strongest support for claims in literary analysis.

quizQuestions.question: A character's motivation refers to:
options: The reasons behind their actions and decisions
The number of scenes they appear in
quizQuestions.answer: The reasons behind their actions and decisions
quizQuestions.explanation: Motivation explains why a character does what they do — their goals, fears, desires, and values that drive their behavior throughout the story.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of a theme statement (not just a topic)?
options: True friendship requires honesty, even when the truth is painful.
The main character has a best friend.
quizQuestions.answer: True friendship requires honesty, even when the truth is painful.
quizQuestions.explanation: A theme is a complete statement expressing a universal idea. 'Friendship' is a topic; the theme must make a claim about that topic that applies beyond the story.

quizQuestions.question: In an argument, the claim is:
options: A debatable position the writer takes and will defend
quizQuestions.answer: A debatable position the writer takes and will defend
quizQuestions.explanation: A claim (or thesis) in an argument is the writer's position on an issue — it must be arguable (not simply a fact) and is the central point the entire essay will support.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is the weakest type of evidence for an argument?
options: A statistic from a peer-reviewed study
A direct quote from an expert in the field
A personal opinion with no supporting data
A real-world example from a credible news source
quizQuestions.answer: A personal opinion with no supporting data
quizQuestions.explanation: Anecdotal or unsupported personal opinions are the weakest form of evidence. Strong arguments rely on facts, data, expert testimony, and documented examples.

quizQuestions.question: In an argument, evidence is used to:
options: Support the claim with facts, examples, or expert information
quizQuestions.answer: Support the claim with facts, examples, or expert information
quizQuestions.explanation: Evidence gives readers a reason to accept the claim, but the writer still needs reasoning to explain how the evidence supports it.

quizQuestions.question: Which evidence is weakest for supporting an argument?
options: A statistic from a peer-reviewed study
A quotation from a relevant expert
A personal opinion with no supporting data
A primary-source document connected to the topic
quizQuestions.answer: A personal opinion with no supporting data
quizQuestions.explanation: Unsupported opinion is weak evidence because it does not give readers verifiable support for the claim.

quizQuestions.question: Which organizational structure presents two subjects by discussing all features of one, then all features of the other?
quizQuestions.answer: Block method
quizQuestions.explanation: The block method of compare-contrast organizes all information about Subject A first, then all information about Subject B. The point-by-point method alternates between subjects for each feature.

quizQuestions.question: Which transition word best signals a cause-and-effect relationship?
quizQuestions.answer: As a result
quizQuestions.explanation: 'As a result' introduces an effect/consequence — it signals that what follows was caused by what was just stated. 'However' and 'in contrast' signal contrast; 'similarly' signals comparison.

quizQuestions.question: An expository essay is primarily meant to:
options: Persuade the reader to change their mind
Entertain the reader with a story
Inform or explain a topic clearly and objectively
quizQuestions.answer: Inform or explain a topic clearly and objectively
quizQuestions.explanation: Expository writing exposes information — its purpose is to explain, describe, or inform without the writer expressing a strong personal opinion or trying to persuade.

quizQuestions.question: Which organizational structure discusses all features of one subject, then all features of another subject?
quizQuestions.answer: Block method
quizQuestions.explanation: The block method presents one subject completely before moving to the second subject.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Which organizational structure works best for an essay about why teen obesity has increased and what consequences it has?
quizQuestions.answer: Cause and effect
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "Cause and effect" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is the most credible source for a research paper on climate science?
options: A personal blog post from an anonymous author
A peer-reviewed journal article published in Nature
A social media post with many likes
quizQuestions.answer: A peer-reviewed journal article published in Nature
quizQuestions.explanation: Peer-reviewed academic journals are considered highly credible because other experts have reviewed and approved the research before publication. Blogs, social media, and promotional sites lack this verification.

quizQuestions.question: Paraphrasing a source means:
options: Copying the original text in quotation marks
Restating the author's ideas in your own words and sentence structure
Summarizing the entire book in one sentence
Using the author's exact words without citation
quizQuestions.answer: Restating the author's ideas in your own words and sentence structure
quizQuestions.explanation: Paraphrasing requires a genuine restatement — new words AND new sentence structure. Simply substituting synonyms while keeping the original structure is still considered plagiarism.

quizQuestions.question: Which source is most credible for a research paper on climate science?
options: A peer-reviewed journal article published in Nature
A personal blog with no citations
A product advertisement about weather equipment
quizQuestions.answer: A peer-reviewed journal article published in Nature
quizQuestions.explanation: A peer-reviewed journal article from a reputable scientific publication is more credible than anonymous, promotional, or unsupported sources.
